sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Brandon, presents with neck pain following a recent car crash in February of this year.
He reports experiencing severe, sharp, and incapacitating pain that is worse with movement.
He has a history of multiple car crashes, with the most recent one occurring in February, for which he had surgery and was in therapy for a few months.
He has been experiencing fatigue since the accident and has been taking fentanyl, but has not received a prescription for several weeks.
He denies any fever, dizziness, visual disturbances, or numbness until recently, when he reported numbness in his left arm and right leg.
He also experiences spasms in his body.
He has been to a pain clinic and seen a therapist, but his current pain is not well-managed.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of multiple car crashes, including surgery and therapy.
He has been to a pain clinic and seen a therapist.
He has a history of taking fentanyl, but has not received a prescription for several weeks.
Review of systems: The patient reports severe, sharp, and incapacitating pain in his neck, with pain radiating to his left arm and right leg.
He experiences fatigue since the accident.
He also reports spasms in his body.
Current medications: Fentanyl (dose and frequency not specified, as he has not received a prescription for several weeks).
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient has moderate range of movement in his neck, with pain on palpation on both the bony process and muscle.
He is unable to bend his neck forward or backwards without pain.
He has no bruising, swelling, or laceration on examination.
Test Results: The patient underwent an x-ray prior to the visit, which showed no fracture.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a neck sprain that is causing severe, sharp, and incapacitating pain.
Problem list: Neck sprain; active; suspected fracture; unknown.
Plan:
I prescribed Robaxin 1500mg every 6-8 hours for pain management.
I advised the patient to apply ice to the area when experiencing bruising and swelling.
I ordered an MRI to further evaluate the extent of the injury.
I referred the patient to physical therapy.
I will refer the patient to pain medicine if the Robaxin is not effective.
I will also consider local injections if the MRI results indicate a need for further treatment.
Follow-up: The patient will follow up with me after the MRI results are available.
